learning.whatWillYouLearn: Prerequisites: Arrays, Loops, HashSet, Time Complexity, Space Complexity
learning.whyDoesThisExist: Imagine checking attendance in a classroom. As each student's ID is read, you record it in a register. If an ID appears again, you immediately know there is a duplicate attendance. There's no need to continue checking the remaining students.
learning.realWorldAnalogy: Imagine checking attendance in a classroom. As each student's ID is read, you record it in a register. If an ID appears again, you immediately know there is a duplicate attendance. There's no need to continue checking the remaining students.
learning.whenToUseThis: Use this Hash Set lookup pattern whenever you need to detect duplicates, repeated values, or previously visited elements efficiently.
learning.signals: Whenever a problem asks whether an element has appeared before, immediately think of using a HashSet.
learning.expectedLearningOutcome: I will know when to use this pattern.

problem.problemStatement: Given an integer array nums, return true if any value appears at least twice in the array, and return false if every element is distinct.

examples.explanation: The value 1 appears more than once.

thinkingProcess.naiveIdea: Compare every element with every other element to determine whether any duplicate exists.
thinkingProcess.keyInsight: Instead of comparing every element with every other element, simply remember the numbers you've already seen. If the current number already exists in the HashSet, a duplicate has been found immediately.
thinkingProcess.ahaMoment: A duplicate means the current element has already been seen.
thinkingProcess.patternRecognition: Whenever a problem asks whether an element has appeared before, immediately think of using a HashSet.
thinkingProcess.whyItWorks: Every element is processed exactly once. HashSet insertion and lookup take O(1) average time, resulting in linear complexity.
thinkingProcess.howToRecognizeSimilar: Whenever a problem asks whether an element has appeared before, immediately think of using a HashSet.
commonWrongPaths: Using a HashMap instead of a HashSet unnecessarily.
Adding before checking when additional information is needed.
Sorting the original array when order must be preserved.
Not returning immediately after finding a duplicate.

hints: A HashSet provides O(1) average lookup.
Return immediately after finding a duplicate.
commonMisconceptions: Using a HashMap instead of a HashSet unnecessarily.
Adding before checking when additional information is needed.
Sorting the original array when order must be preserved.
Not returning immediately after finding a duplicate.

pattern.name: Hash Set Lookup
pattern.recognitionSignals: Whenever a problem asks whether an element has appeared before, immediately think of using a HashSet.

approaches.title: Nested Loop Comparison
approaches.idea: Compare every element with every other element to determine whether any duplicate exists.
algorithm: Loop j from i+1 to n-1.
Return false after checking every pair.
approaches.dryRun:
nums=[1,2,3,1]
Compare 1 with 2
Compare 1 with 3
Compare 1 with 1 -> Duplicate Found

approaches.title: Sorting
approaches.idea: Sort the array first, then check neighboring elements for duplicates.
algorithm: Compare current element with previous element.

approaches.title: HashSet Lookup
approaches.idea: Traverse the array once while storing every visited number inside a HashSet. If the current number already exists in the set, immediately return true.
approaches.intuition: Every element is processed exactly once. HashSet insertion and lookup take O(1) average time, resulting in linear complexity.
algorithm: If current number exists in the HashSet, return true.
Otherwise insert the number into the HashSet.
approaches.dryRun:
nums=[1,2,3,1]
Set={}
1 -> Add
2 -> Add
3 -> Add
1 -> Already Exists -> Return true

visualization.description: The HashSet stores every number that has already appeared.

interviewFollowUps: Can this be solved without extra space?
How would you count duplicate frequencies?
What if duplicates must be within distance k?
What if the input is a data stream?
How would you return the duplicated values instead of true/false?
interviewTips: Explain why a HashSet is sufficient instead of a HashMap.
Return immediately after finding a duplicate.
Discuss the sorting alternative if extra space is restricted.

cheatSheet.keyObservation: A duplicate means the current element has already been seen.
cheatSheet.algorithm: Lookup in HashSet → If found return true → Else insert.

commonMistakes: Using a HashMap instead of a HashSet unnecessarily.
Adding before checking when additional information is needed.
Sorting the original array when order must be preserved.
Not returning immediately after finding a duplicate.
